statement.markdown: Solve the problem and fix the bugs.
statement.inputFormat: A list of strings to be inserted into the Trie, and the number of strings to be inserted.
statement.outputFormat: The number of strings that were inserted into the Trie.
statement.notes: Assume that all strings are composed of lowercase English letters.

constraints.description: Number of strings to be inserted into the Trie

examples.explanation: The first string 'a' is inserted because it's not present in the Trie. The second string 'b' is also inserted, even though its 'b' is not a new insertion because the Trie only stores the end of each string and 'b c' is the end of a string but not the end of the first string it's in. The last string 'c' is inserted, as it's not present in the Trie.

hints.text: Review the implementation of the insert function in the Trie class.

editorial.title: Fixing Trie Insertion Edge Case:
editorial.intuition:
The key challenge in this problem is to understand the purpose of the Trie and how it stores information.
A Trie, also known as a prefix tree, is a tree-like data structure in which every node stores a string. Each node represents a prefix of all the strings that branch off from it. The key idea is to insert strings into the Trie such that each string's edges are a distinct path.
One common implementation of a Trie includes a class with methods for inserting words, removing words, and searching for words.
editorial.bruteForce:
A brute-force solution would simply traverse the graph to see whether each string is present. This brute-force method has a time complexity of O(n*m) because it involves inserting each character in each string into the Trie, which results in a total of (n*m) operations, where n is the number of strings to be inserted (or searched for) and m is the maximum length of a string.
However, we can improve upon this method by implementing the Trie as a data structure that includes a boolean array of size 26 representing the next nodes for each character in the Trie.
editorial.betterSolution: To solve this problem efficiently, you should implement a Trie class that includes methods to insert words, remove words, and search for words. You should ensure that each word's edges are a distinct path in the Trie, i.e., that there is at most one string that matches a prefix in the Trie. The key challenge in this problem is to handle this requirement in the implementation of the insert method.
editorial.optimalSolution: The optimal solution involves implementing the Trie class with a method to insert words. The key idea is to maintain a boolean array of size 26 in the Trie node, representing the next nodes for each character. When inserting a word, we should avoid overwriting existing nodes in the Trie. This can be achieved by traversing the Trie and ensuring that there is at most one string that matches a prefix in the Trie.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: To demonstrate the correctness of our implementation, we can add a method to print the content of the Trie after each insertion. This will help us verify that our implementation handles the edge case correctly.
editorial.dryRun: Consider the string 'abc' and 'a b'. If we insert them into the Trie separately, we should get different paths for these two strings.
commonMistakes: Not handling the case where there is an existing node in the Trie that matches the start of the new string to be inserted.
Not ensuring that there is at most one string that matches a prefix in the Trie.
